turns.content: okay so random question but do you know if stretching before or after a workout is better?? I keep getting conflicting info online and my instructor says one thing but then this physio account on instagram says the opposite

Great question! The short answer is that both have their place, but they serve different purposes:

**Before a workout:** Dynamic stretching is recommended — things like leg swings, arm circles, gentle lunges. These warm up the muscles and increase blood flow without overstretching cold tissue.

**After a workout:** Static stretching is better here — holding a stretch for 20-30 seconds. This helps with flexibility and muscle recovery when the tissue is already warm.

The outdated advice was to do long static stretches before exercise, but research shows that can actually reduce muscle performance temporarily. What kind of workout are you doing? That might help me give more specific advice.

pilates! I go 3 times a week at this place on Al Reem Island. its reformer pilates so theres a lot of like controlled stretching built into the exercises already but I always wonder if I should be doing more on my own before class starts

Reformer Pilates is great because, as you said, it already incorporates a lot of controlled lengthening and stretching into the movements. The reformer springs provide resistance that naturally warms you up as you go.

That said, if you're arriving at class and jumping straight onto the reformer, a 5-minute dynamic warm-up beforehand can help — especially for your hip flexors and thoracic spine. Something like:

- Cat-cow stretches (30 seconds)
- Gentle torso rotations
- Hip circles
- Shoulder rolls

After class, a few minutes of static stretching for your hip flexors and hamstrings would be beneficial. Do you find any particular area feels tight during class?

my back and neck tbh. I'm hunched over a desk literally all day at work drawing sections and details and by the time I get to class my upper back is just. a disaster. that's actually the main reason I started pilates in the first place, my posture was getting so bad

That makes a lot of sense. Desk work — especially detailed drawing — puts your body in a sustained forward-flexed position: head forward, shoulders rounded, upper back (thoracic spine) in constant flexion. Over time, the muscles in the front of your chest and neck get tight, while the ones in your upper back get weak.

Pilates is one of the best things you can do for this because it targets exactly those postural muscles. A few things that might help on top of your classes:

1. **Thoracic extension over a foam roller** — lie lengthwise on it for 2-3 minutes at the end of the day
2. **Chin tucks** — do them at your desk every hour (just pull your chin straight back, hold 5 seconds)
3. **Doorway chest stretches** — arms on the doorframe, lean through gently

How long have you been doing the 3x/week schedule?

like 4 months now! it costs 250 dirhams a month which I mean its not cheap for someone on a junior architect salary but wallah my spine needed the intervention. I was getting headaches from the tension in my neck and everything. so I figure 250 a month is cheaper than physiotherapy appointments right

Absolutely — 250 dirhams a month for three sessions a week is actually very reasonable when you frame it that way. A single physiotherapy session in Abu Dhabi can run 300-500 dirhams, so you'd blow through that monthly budget in one visit.

And the fact that your headaches have improved tells you it's working. Tension headaches from neck and upper trapezius tightness are incredibly common in people who do detailed desk work, and they respond really well to the kind of postural correction Pilates provides.

Four months is right around the time when the benefits start compounding — your body is building the postural endurance to hold itself better during the workday, not just during class. Have you noticed any improvement at your desk too, or mostly just during and after sessions?

actually yes!! my colleague pointed out that I sit straighter now which was like the best compliment anyone has given me lol. I still catch myself hunching when I'm deep in a detail drawing but at least I notice it now? before pilates I didn't even realize how bad it was. I was just living in goblin posture 24/7

"Goblin posture" — I think a lot of desk workers would relate to that description!

The fact that you now notice when you're hunching is actually one of the most important changes. That's called proprioceptive awareness — your body has relearned what good alignment feels like, so the bad posture now feels wrong. Before, it felt normal because it was your default.

The deep-focus hunching is harder to fix because when your brain is absorbed in detail work, it deprioritizes postural signals. A couple of tricks:

- Set a gentle timer every 30 minutes to do a quick posture check
- Keep your monitor or drawing surface slightly higher than feels natural — it forces you to sit up
- Do a quick 10-second shoulder blade squeeze whenever you catch yourself

You're making great progress. The colleague noticing is a sign that the change is visible, not just felt.

the timer thing is smart I'm gonna try that. I get so locked into drawings sometimes I don't move for like 2 hours straight its terrible. okay last thing — do you think pilates alone is enough or should I add something else? like I don't do any cardio at all rn

Pilates is excellent for core strength, flexibility, and postural correction, but you're right that it doesn't cover cardiovascular fitness. Ideally, a well-rounded routine includes:

1. **Strength/flexibility** — your Pilates covers this well
2. **Cardiovascular exercise** — something that elevates your heart rate for 20-30 minutes
3. **General movement** — walking, taking stairs, etc.

You don't need anything intense. Even adding 2-3 brisk walks per week (20-30 minutes) would make a meaningful difference. Swimming is another great option in Abu Dhabi — low impact, works the whole body, and counters the desk-flexion pattern since many strokes put you in extension.

But honestly, if budget and time are factors, your currentAtes routine is doing a lot of good. Don't let the pursuit of a perfect routine undermine a good one that you're actually sticking to. Three sessions a week consistently is better than a complicated plan you abandon in a month.

true true. I think I'll stick with what I have for now and maybe add walks on the weekend or something. yalla I need to get back to this facade drawing before my senior architect comes looking for it lol. thanks for all the stretching advice!!

Good plan — weekend walks would be a perfect low-pressure addition. And definitely try those chin tucks and the doorway stretch during the work week. Your spine will thank you.

Good luck with the facade drawing — go sit up straight while you do it! Hope the Pilates keeps helping.
